C. The right hilum contains the right pulmonary veins, so they are at T5-T7.
D. Origin of ascending aorta is at around T5, slightly lower than the lower end of the aortic arch at T4/5 intervertebral disc.

16-17 MEDU2300 Final Q1.question: Which of the following muscle posterior to the internal thoracic artery links sternum to ribs?
16-17 MEDU2300 Final Q1.explanation:
![](img/16-17_MEDU2300_Final_Q1_1775752599053.png)

The problem lies not in "muscle posterior to the internal thoracic artery".
Internal thoracic artery is a branch of the subclavian artery that runs directly behind the first 6 costal cartilage between transversus thoracis and internal intercostal muscles. It terminates at the sixth thoracic vertebrae and gives rise to musculophrenic artery and superior epigastric artery.
The problem lies in which type of muscle "links sternum to ribs". Obviously, only transversus thoracis does that.

16-17 MEDU2300 Final Q3.question: The intercostal vessels and nerves course through which layers of tissues?
16-17 MEDU2300 Final Q3.explanation:
![](img/16-17_MEDU2300_Final_Q3_1775752618555.png)
![](img/16-17_MEDU2300_Final_Q3_1775752627354.png)

View the thorax from a transverse section. (P. 20 of lecture notes or Atlas)
Except for the lateral cutaneous branch, the intercostal VAN runs along the space between internal intercostal muscle and innermost intercostal muscle. This is because the intercostal VAN and the internal thoracic artery travels in the same layer.
The following 2 photos show many other important relations (e.g. internal thoracic artery), which are useful for a lot of questions that follow. Please refer to them whenever necessary.

16-17 MEDU2300 Final Q5.question: Which of the following structures links to the manubriosternal joint?
16-17 MEDU2300 Final Q5.explanation:
manubriosternal joint = sternal angle
Try not to be confused by the "head" options.

16-17 MEDU2300 Final Q6.question: Which of the following structures is the major motor nerve supply to the diaphragm?

options.A: Cervical sympathetic and lower intercostal nerves
options.D: Phrenic nerve and lower intercostal nerves

16-17 MEDU2300 Final Q6.explanation:
It is not just major, but is the ONLY SOMATIC MOTOR innervation.
The central part of the diaphragm receives sensory fibres from the phrenic nerve, whereas the peripheral part receives sensory fibers from the intercostal nerves.
D. Lower intercostal nerve is sensory

17-18 MEDU2300 Test Q1.question: Which muscle attaches the sternum to the ribs

17-18 MEDU2300 Test Q2.question: A superficial cut is made on the lateral side of the sternum. Which vessel would be damaged?
17-18 MEDU2300 Test Q2.explanation: Internal thoracic artery runs along the parasternal line, which is on the midway between the lateral sternal line and mid-clavicular line.

17-18 MEDU2300 Final Q1.question: which of the following is the anatomical characteristic of T4

options.C: Costosternal junction with the fourth ribs

17-18 MEDU2300 Final Q1.explanation: To be exact, it should be the beginning and ending of the aortic arch, not the entire aortic arch.

18-19 MEDU2300 Test Q1.question: On male chest surface, where is the apex of the heart located?

options.A: At the level of left costal margin, 3.5 inch from the midline
options.B: At the level of left nipple
options.C: At the level of 4th intercostal space, 3.5 inch from the midline
options.D: At the level of 5th intercostal space, 3.5 inch from the midline
options.E: At the level of 6th intercostal space, 3.5 inch. from midline

18-19 MEDU2300 Test Q1.explanation:
![](img/18-19_MEDU2300_Test_Q1_1775752687360.png)

It is the exact location that you can hear the auscultation of the bicuspid valve.

18-19 MEDU2300 Test Q2.question: Which of the following does not supply the breast?

options.B: Internal thoracic artery and its branches

19-20 MEDU2300 Test Q1.question: What can be found behind the sternal angle?
19-20 MEDU2300 Test Q1.explanation: B. inferior border of superior mediastinum

19-20 MEDU2300 Test Q2.question: For thoracentesis, inject something into the lung through the lateral side of the chest wall, which is the first layer of muscle to be penetrated by the needle
19-20 MEDU2300 Test Q2.explanation:
Thoracentesis is done on the lateral side of the body. Note the position of the safety triangle, which is formed medially by the pectoralis major muscle, inferiorly by the base of diaphragm, laterally by the latissimus dorsi (posterior axillary line). Serratus anterior is the most superior layer of muscle.

Well, this may not be scientifically correct. Refer to the photo below. Moore depicts the procedure of thoracocentesis using the 9th intercostal space along the midaxillary line. Note that serratus anterior runs only along the lateral surface first 8 ribs from the medial side of scapula. Therefore, serratus anterior is not even present in this area that thoracocentesis is performed! The answer has to be external intercostal muscles in this case. [2]

![](img/19-20_MEDU2300_Test_Q2_1775752729966.png)

In the next question, there is another diagram given in a very old lecture of Franky Chan. The safety triangle (in Dr. Calvin Ng’s surface anatomy lecture, most textbooks and online sources), is defined with an imaginary line from the nipple as the inferior border. In other words, the inferior border is somewhere around the 4th intercostal space. Franky defines the diaphragm as “moving around the 5th and 6th rib”, which is within the 5th intercostal space. The main point is not the difference between the 2 definitions of the location of diaphragm, but whether the safety triangle is designed for thoracocentesis. Given that usually thoracocentesis is performed at 7th-9th intercostal space (to avoid the liver, spleen, diaphragm and lungs), which is outside of this safety triangle, how can this define the location for thoracocentesis?

The safety triangle can only be said to be the best site for chest drain, particularly at the 4th intercostal space.

The main problem is: Is “chest drain” equivalent to “thoracocentesis”?

19-20 MEDU2300 Final Q2.question: Intercostal nerves can be injured by injection by needle. Which of the following places of injection would most likely injure intercostal bundles?
19-20 MEDU2300 Final Q2.explanation:
![](img/19-20_MEDU2300_Final_Q2_1775752749556.png)

Keep close to the superior border of the ribs as intercostal VAN is found near the inferior border (the groove).

20-21 MEDU2300 Test Q1.question: Which of the following is most likely found in the horizontal section cut at sternal angle?

options.C: The inferior border of the superior mediastinum
options.D: Intervertebral disc between T2 and T3 vertebrae

20-21 MEDU2300 Test Q1.explanation:
A. Trachea begins at the cricoid cartilage (C6) and runs downwards. It just branches into the left and right bronchus at the T4 level.
B. Brachiocephalic trunk is found at T2
C. Correct. The sternal angle marks the transverse thoracic plane, which divides the mediastinum into superior and 3 remaining parts of the mediastinum.
D. Sternal angle is at T4
E. Top of aortic arch is found at T2

20-21 MEDU2300 Test Q2.question: When a superficial cut is made on the lateral sternum, which of the following blood vessels will be damaged?
20-21 MEDU2300 Test Q2.explanation:
A. Bronchial arteries are branches from the thoracic aorta or intercostal arteries. It is found in the posterior mediastinum.
B. There are multiple sources of blood supply to the esophagus. Thoracic oesophagus is supplied by some direct branches from the thoracic aorta. These are found in the posterior mediastinum.
In the abdomen, the blood supply include the oesophageal branches from the left gastric artery (celiac trunk), and the inferior phrenic artery (a direct branch from the abdominal aorta). (For future reference)
There are also some cervical supply, but those names are probably not important at this stage.
C. Although this is the answer as you memorise the past exam questions, you should figure out what the other arteries are and where they can be found.
D. This does not exist.
E. The pericardiocophrenic artery is a branch from the internal thoracic artery. It accompanies the phrenic nerve between the pleura and the pericardium to the diaphragm. In other words, it is located deeper relatively.

20-21 MEDU2300 Final Q1.question: A guy was stabbed on the 5th intercostal space right to the sternum. The stab wound was 1cm deep. Which one of the structures is most likely injured?
20-21 MEDU2300 Final Q1.explanation: Recall that apex of heart is at 5th ICS at midclavicular line, and considering the leftward orientation of heart, 5th ICS right sternal border is already away from the heart. Also, the 1cm wound can only damage the thoracic wall. C, D and E are all impossible to be injured. B is correct as again, internal thoracic artery roughly runs along the sternal border and sternocostal joint.

20-21 MEDU2300 Final Q2.question: After the surgical removal of the mammary tumour mass in the right breast of the patient, the patient had difficulty to elevate her right arm above the horizontal plane. Which of the following nerves is likely to be injured?
20-21 MEDU2300 Final Q2.explanation:
Cannot elevate arm above horizontal plane = no glenoid up → serratus anterior gg → long thoracic nerve gg. This question is directly taken from Gray’s review, the answer is long thoracic nerve.

![](img/20-21_MEDU2300_Final_Q2_1775752860910.png)
![](img/20-21_MEDU2300_Final_Q2_1775752844793.png)

21-22 MEDU2300 Test Q1.question: Which of the following articulates with the manubriosternal joint?
21-22 MEDU2300 Test Q1.explanation: A is called sternoclavicular joint, which you will learn in Back and Upper Limb. Always remember the head of rib articulates posteriorly with the costal facet of vertebrae, while the costal cartilage articulates anteriorly with the sternum.

21-22 MEDU2300 Test Q2.question: The intercostal neurovascular bundle travels between which two layers?
21-22 MEDU2300 Test Q2.explanation: In previous questions, it is said that the intercostal VAN travels along the inner interocostal muscles and transversus thoracis. However, you can read small Moore to read the exact course as it travels as transversus thoracis inserts at the inner surface of costal cartilages 2-6, and the relations would change slightly. You should be able to get this answer right anyways.

21-22 MEDU2300 Final Q1.question: The anatomical relationship of the neurovascular bundle within the intercostal space from superior to inferior is:
21-22 MEDU2300 Final Q1.explanation: Just memorize VAN! This also works for contents in the femoral triangle (From medial to lateral), but not popliteal fossa (you will see them soon).

21-22 MEDU2300 Final Q2.question: The internal thoracic artery gives rise to the following branches except:
21-22 MEDU2300 Final Q2.explanation:
Internal thoracic artery comes from the subclavian artery, then descends along the parasternal line to give out anterior intercostal arteries and internal mammary artery. At the costal margin (7th costal cartilage), it divides into musculophrenic artery (Running along costal margin) and superior epigastric artery (Descends into rectus sheath to supply rectus abdominis and anastomose with inferior epigastric artery).
3rd – 11th posterior intercostal arteries come from thoracic aorta directly, while the first two come from costocervical trunk of subclavian artery. Be careful there are 9 anterior intercostal arteries, but 11 on posterior sides. This is because the floating ribs (11th and 12th) do not extend to the anterior thoracic wall.

22-23 MEDU2300 Test Q1.question: Female breasts least likely to be supplied by

22-23 MEDU2300 Test Q2.question: For thoracentesis, insert chest drain into the lung through the lateral side of the chest wall, which is the first layer of muscle to be penetrated by the needle?

FA MEDU2300 Thorax Q1.question: The 5th intercostal artery of the anterior thoracic wall arises from:
FA MEDU2300 Thorax Q1.explanation:
All anterior intercostal arteries arise from internal thoracic artery, which arises from 1st part of subclavian artery in the neck and descends behind the first 6 costal cartilages.
Superior epigastric and musculophrenic arteries are the terminal branches of internal thoracic artery, arising at the costal margin at approximately the 6th intercostal space. Thoracic aorta gives the 3rd – 11th posterior intercostal arteries instead.
The 1st and 2nd posterior intercostal arteries arise from the superior intercostal artery, which is from costocervical trunk of subclavian artery. (Head & Neck content)

FA MEDU2300 Thorax Q2.question: Which of the following dermatome are the nipples within?
FA MEDU2300 Thorax Q2.explanation: T1 is in medial part of arm. T4 is in the thorax at the level of nipples. T6 (Or T7) is the level of xiphoid process (xiphosternum). T10 is at the level of umbilicus, which is also the site of referred pain of acute appendicitis, make sure you know this. T12 (Preferably L1) is for the pubic region.

FA MEDU2300 Thorax Q3.question: How many pairs of internal intercostal muscles are there?
FA MEDU2300 Thorax Q3.explanation: You have 12 pairs of ribs, then you will have 11 ICS and 11 pairs of intercostals.

FA MEDU2300 Thorax Q4.question: In what direction do external intercostal muscles run on posterior thoracic wall?
FA MEDU2300 Thorax Q4.explanation: External intercostals run obliquely downward and medially at the anterior wall（插袋）, so as the pattern moves to the back, it will become obliquely downward and laterally. Draw it out to understand this if you cannot visualize.

FA MEDU2300 Thorax Q5.question: What can be palpated at both sides of the sternal angle?

options.B: Intercostal muscles of the 2nd intercostal space
options.E: Intercostal muscles of the 1st intercostal space

FA MEDU2300 Thorax Q5.explanation: Sternal angle is the manubriosternal joint (T4), which is also the articulation between 2nd costal cartilages to the sternum.

FA MEDU2300 Thorax Q6.question: Which one of the following structures form a part of the thoracic inlet (superior thoracic aperture)?
FA MEDU2300 Thorax Q6.explanation: Thoracic inlet is formed by the first ribs, clavicular and jugular notches of manubrium, and T1 vertebra.

Extra MEDU2300 Thorax Q1.question: Which of the following concerning the thorax is correct?

options.A: The arch of the aorta begins at the level of the jugular notch.
options.B: The internal thoracic artery branches off to give the musculophrenic artery and the superficial epigastric artery.
options.C: Thoracocentesis should be performed near the superior border of a rib.
options.E: The intercostal vessels and nerves course through external and internal intercostal muscles.

Extra MEDU2300 Thorax Q1.explanation:
A. The arch of aorta is located at T4.
B. Superficial → Superior epigastric artery
C. Correct
D. There are 11 intercostal spaces.
E. The intercostal VAN courses through the internal and innermost intercostal muscles.

15-16 MEDU2300 Final Q3.question: Which of the following statements most accurately describes the oesophageal hiatus on the diaphragm?

options.A: It encircles the oesophagus and the left phrenic nerve
options.B: It can be narrowed by the stimulation of the vagus nerve
options.D: It is located in the centre of the central tendon of the diaphragm
options.E: It lies anteriorly and to the left of the aortic orifice

15-16 MEDU2300 Final Q3.explanation:
![](img/15-16_MEDU2300_Final_Q3_1775809286150.jpeg)
![](img/15-16_MEDU2300_Final_Q3_1775809296617.png)

Oesophageal hiatus at T10 is on the left and anterior aspect of aortic hiatus, which is along the midline. It is created by a loop of the right crux of the diaphragm. Apart from esophagus and vagus nerve, the left inferior phrenic vessels and small esophageal arteries from left gastric vessels also go through the hiatus.
Vena caval opening is at T8 and on the central tendon of diaphragm.
Oesophageal hiatus is formed by diaphragm, which does not receive innervation by CN X. However, it is the part of LES within the oesophagus being constricted under PSNS stimulation. Oesophageal hiatus carries CN X instead of phrenic nerve.
In thorax, the aortic hiatus is seldom talked about. Note that the aorta goes below the median arcuate ligament of the diaphragm (diaphragm attachment; middle one). Slightly below this level, the aorta can be cross-clamped during abdominal surgery, such as during abdominal aortic aneurysm repair. (Clinical significance taught by Paul Lai)
One can remember this with the mnemonic "I 8 10 EGGs AAT 12": I 8 = IVC at T8. 10 EGGs = EsophaGus and vaGus at T10. AAT 12 = Aorta, Azygos, and Thoracic duct at T12. (Also check out other mnemonics in Gabriel’s Notes)

15-16 MEDU2300 Final Q5.question: Which of the following statement about trachea is incorrect?

options.A: it has the arch of aorta on its left side
options.B: it is closely related to the left recurrent laryngeal nerve
options.C: it is on the left side of the midline
options.D: it is crossed by the left brachiocephalic vein
options.E: it is crossed by the brachiocephalic trunk

15-16 MEDU2300 Final Q5.explanation:
![](img/15-16_MEDU2300_Final_Q5_1775809321637.png)

A. The arch of aorta arches over the right pulmonary artery and the left main bronchus
B. The left recurrent laryngeal nerve is in between the arch of aorta and the left main bronchus
C. It lies exactly on the midline in the posterior mediastinum together with the esophagus, except that the distal part of esophagus is displaced to the left as it transverses through the esophageal hiatus to the stomach.
D. Left brachiocephalic vein and brachiocephalic trunk cross midline, so they cross the trachea.

15-16 MEDU2300 Final Q6.question: Which of the following statements with regard to the pleural cavity is incorrect?

options.A: It is bound by the parietal pleura.
options.B: The parietal pleura has sensory innervations.
options.D: It is not occupied by the lung
options.E: It extends below medial end of the twelve rib

15-16 MEDU2300 Final Q6.explanation:
![](img/15-16_MEDU2300_Final_Q6_1775809340056.png)
![](img/15-16_MEDU2300_Final_Q6_1775809345402.png)

A. Pleural cavity is bound by BOTH parietal pleura and visceral pleura.
B. This is a reference that is MADE UP! Yet, this is an important concept to know that the parietal pleura can sense pain!
C. Refer to the right figure below. (Apices is 2 cm above the clavicles)
D. Correct as it is filled with pleural fluid
E. Refer to the left figure below.
Keep in mind the 8-10-12 rule of inferior limits of parietal pleura: 8th rib at midclavicular line, 10th at midaxillary line, 12th at scapular line and body of T12 vertebra. Body of T12 vertebra is slightly lower than medial end of 12th rib, which is the transverse process of T12. (Surface projection)

15-16 MEDU2300 Test Q8.question: Which of the following supplies the main motor function of diaphragm?

options.D: lesser thoracic splanchnic And phrenic nerves

15-16 MEDU2300 Test Q8.explanation: A is referring to the sensory innervation of diaphragm. Lower intercostal nerves supply the peripheral part and phrenic nerve supplies the central part.

15-16 MEDU2300 Test Q16.question: The sound of the mitral valve can be best heard at which of the following sites?

options.A: the fifth intercostal space near the apex of the heart
options.B: the back near the scapular line
options.C: the lateral margin of the sternum at the level of the sternal angle
options.D: the left midclavicular line at the level of the sternal angle of Louis
options.E: the superior border of the heart

15-16 MEDU2300 Test Q20.question: With reference to the coronary circulation, which of the following statements is INCORRECT?

options.A: The right coronary artery is closer to the sternum than the left coronary artery.
options.B: When compared with other regions of the body, the heart has a minimum of anastomotic vessels.
options.C: Minute veins in the heart wall may drain directly into the heart chambers.
options.D: The atrioventricular nodal artery usually arises from the right coronary artery.
options.E: The right coronary artery is shorter than the left coronary artery.

15-16 MEDU2300 Test Q20.explanation:
![](img/15-16_MEDU2300_Test_Q20_1775809414320.png)

A. Without recalling any direct statements from the lectures, just think about it. Your heart is located slightly to the left of the midline. The coronary artery is the first branch of ascending aorta. Obviously, the coronary artery on the right will be closer to your sternum in the midline.
B. There are 2 anastomotic vessels in your heart: a. The anterior interventricular artery & posterior interventricular artery; b. The right coronary artery and the left circumflex artery
C. There is only 1 type of cardiac veins that begin in the heart wall and empty DIRECTLY into its chambers, and that is the smallest cardiac veins.
D. This is true. The right coronary artery also gives rise to SA nodal artery, posterior interventricular artery and marginal artery
E. The left coronary artery is shorter than the right coronary artery as it branches off very soon into anterior interventricular artery and circumflex artery.

15-16 MEDU2300 Final Q2.question: Which of the following concerning the mediastinum is correct?

options.A: The superior mediastinum contains the remnant of thyroid gland
options.B: The vagus nerves pass from superior mediastinum to middle mediastinum
options.C: The superior mediastinum is behind the body of sternum
options.D: The root of aorta is amongst the content of middle mediastinum

15-16 MEDU2300 Final Q2.explanation:
A. The remnant of thyroid gland is in the ANTERIOR mediastinum. However, the superior mediastinum does contain the thymus. 
B. It (vagus nerve) descends on the left side of the aortic arch, which separates it from the left pleura, and travels behind the phrenic nerve. It courses behind the root of the left lung and then deviates medially and downwards to reach the esophagus and form the esophageal plexus by joining the opposite (right) vagus nerve.
a. In short, the vagus nerve should be going into the POSTERIOR mediastinum instead of the middle mediastinum.
C. The sternal angle is already the inferior border of superior mediastinum. It should be posterior to the manubrium.
D. Middle mediastinum contains the roots of all great vessels.

15-16 MEDU2300 Final Q4.question: Which of the following statements with regards to the heart valves is correct?

options.A: The aortic valve is found at the right medial end of the 2nd intercostal space.
options.B: The tricuspid valve can be heard at the 5th intercostal space on the left.
options.C: The aortic valve and pulmonary valves are too close that cannot be distinguished.
options.D: The valve of superior vena cava can be heard.
options.E: Aortic sinus forms at each cusp of the aortic valves.

15-16 MEDU2300 Final Q4.explanation:
![](img/15-16_MEDU2300_Final_Q4_1775809487166.png)
![](img/15-16_MEDU2300_Final_Q4_1775809493170.png)

A. The aortic valve, obliquely placed, is located posterior to the left side of the sternum at the level of the 3rd intercostal space (small moore). Option A is only true if it talks about where to hear the aortic valve sound.
B. Tricuspid → Mitral
C. Can be distinguished
D. Cannot; in fact it is more correct to say that SVC has no valves
E. The aortic sinuses are the spaces at the origin of the ascending aorta between the dilated wall of the vessel and each cusp of the aortic (semilunar) valve. The opening of the right coronary artery is in the right aortic sinus; the opening of the left coronary artery is in the left aortic sinus; and no artery arises from the posterior aortic (noncoronary) sinus. (small moore)

15-16 MEDU2300 Final Q7.question: Which of the following about aortic arch is correct?

options.A: move from left to right when it arches the pulmonary artery
options.C: highest border is at the same level of jugular notch
options.D: related inferiorly with the left main bronchus
options.E: branches 2 subclavian arteries and 2 common carotid arteries

15-16 MEDU2300 Final Q7.explanation:
![](img/15-16_MEDU2300_Final_Q7_1775809515554.png)

A. right to left instead
B. Root of the neck refers to the thoracic outlet, which is bounded by the first thoracic vertebra, the first pair of ribs and their cartilages and the manubrium of the sternum. Usually, the main structures are those subclavian arteries, brachiocephalic trunks etc. They are above the arch of aorta.
C. highest border: T3/4 VS jugular notch T2/3
D. The left main bronchus runs inferior to the arch of aorta
E. 1 left subclavian and 1 left common carotid artery; 1 brachiocephalic trunk branch into right subclavian and common carotid

15-16 MEDU2300 Final Q8.question: With regard to the cardiac conduction system, which of the following statements is correct?

options.A: SA node is located at the junction between IVC and right atrium
options.B: Septomarginal band carries left conducting nerves
options.C: AV bundle of his is located anterior to the membrane of the interventricular septum
options.D: AV node located close to the opening of the coronary sinus
options.E: AV node is located between the superior vena cava and auricle

15-16 MEDU2300 Final Q8.explanation:
![](img/15-16_MEDU2300_Final_Q8_1775809537796.png)
![](img/15-16_MEDU2300_Final_Q8_1775809544268.png)
![](img/15-16_MEDU2300_Final_Q8_1775809553364.png)

A. SA node lies in the myocardium at the upper end of the crista terminalis near the opening of the SVC in the right atrium
B. Septomarginal band = moderator band = right bundle of conducting system → from right ventricular apex to the anterior papillary muscle
C. AV node lies in the septal wall of right atrium, superior and medial to the opening of the coronary sinus in the right atrium
D. AV node is located in the Koch’s triangle. One of the borders of the triangle is formed by the coronary sinus. AV node should be medial to the opening of the coronary sinus. These 2 links are important.
E. This is for the SA node.

15-16 MEDU2300 Final Q11.question: Which of the following is featured in LV?
15-16 MEDU2300 Final Q11.explanation:
A. Obviously true
B. For both left and right ventricles.
C. ONLY in RIGHT ventricle
D. On both left and right atria
E. On all heart valves

16-17 MEDU2300 Test Q5.question: Which of the following passes through the aortic hiatus of the diaphragm?
16-17 MEDU2300 Test Q5.explanation:
The aortic hiatus has the aorta, thoracic duct, azygos vein and occasionally the greater splanchnic nerve.
C passes through the muscle fibers of the diaphragm.

16-17 MEDU2300 Test Q6.question: CT scan shows a tumor superior to the right root of lung. Which blood circulation will most likely be affected?
16-17 MEDU2300 Test Q6.explanation:
![](img/16-17_MEDU2300_Test_Q6_1775809602709.png)

Note that the azygos vein enters the thoracic cavity via the aortic hiatus and arches over the root of the right lung to the SVC.
Refer to the diagram. E may be a possible answer if the tumor develops anteriorly.

16-17 MEDU2300 Test Q9.question: With reference to the rotation of the foregut along the longitudinal axis, which is the orientation of the left vagus nerve in relation to the esophagus?

options.D: on the left lateral side of esophagus
options.E: on the right lateral side of esophagus

16-17 MEDU2300 Test Q9.explanation:
Learn it from Paul Lai, "Google it!"
Just kidding. This will be covered in the anatomy of the abdomen / GI embryology.

16-17 MEDU2300 Final Q2.question: Which of the following goes through esophageal hiatus?
16-17 MEDU2300 Final Q2.explanation:
One possible way to recite this can be: Vagus nerve = parasympathetic = related to GI = oesophageal hiatus
For your reference, C is wrong as the branch of the right phrenic nerve traverses the caval opening to supply the back of the liver. This is why inflammation of liver and gallbladder may be referred to the C3-C5 dermatome at the shoulder.

16-17 MEDU2300 Final Q7.question: With regards to the main bronchi, which of the following statements is correct?

options.A: The right one is longer than the left one
options.B: The right one forms a smaller angle when it enters the lung/when it branches from the trachea
options.C: The left one is longer than the right one
options.D: The right one lies in front of the esophagus
options.E: The left and right are of equal length

16-17 MEDU2300 Final Q7.explanation:
![](img/16-17_MEDU2300_Final_Q7_1775809638061.png)

Right bronchus
- has a larger diameter but shorter length.
- is more vertical → food might fall into the right bronchus more easily.
- enters the lungs at a higher level of T5 (while left bronchus enters left lung at T6)
Note that the right one forms a larger angle while it enters the lung because it is more vertical while the hilum is horizontal.

16-17 MEDU2300 Final Q11.question: Esophageal opening on diaphragm

options.B: Surrounded by a sling of fiber of right crus
options.D: Transmit branches of right gastric art.
options.E: Transmit branches of right phrenic n.

16-17 MEDU2300 Final Q11.explanation:
(Refer to Lecture: [Organization of abdomen and pelvis] P.8)
A. T8 → T10 level
C. In muscular right crus
D. Right gastric → left gastric artery (esophageal branches)
E. phrenic → vagus (right phrenic nerve can be found at the venal caval opening)

16-17 MEDU2300 Test Q1.question: In case of injury or congenital defect of the interventricular septum, which of the following valves is most likely affected?
16-17 MEDU2300 Test Q1.explanation:
D. defect in ventricular septum — leakage of blood to RV due to higher pressure in LV — higher pressures in RV → Tricuspid regurgitation
Google Sensei: https://www.msdmanuals.com/professional/pediatrics/congenital-cardiovascular-anomalies/ventricular-septal-defect-vsd

16-17 MEDU2300 Test Q3.question: Posterior interventricular septum damage may be due to blockage of
16-17 MEDU2300 Test Q3.explanation:
The anterior ? of the IV septum is supplied by the anterior interventricular artery from the left coronary artery.
The posterior ? of the IV septum is supplied by the posterior interventricular artery from the right coronary artery.

16-17 MEDU2300 Final Q8.question: Which of the following loops around the aortic arch next to the ligamentum arteriosum?
16-17 MEDU2300 Final Q8.explanation:
To be precise, the left recurrent laryngeal nerve loops around the aortic arch POSTERIOR to the ligamentum arteriosum.
The ligamentum arteriosum is a small ligament that attaches the aortic arch to the pulmonary artery. It has no special functions, just a remnant of the ductus arteriosus.

16-17 MEDU2300 Final Q9.question: Location of SA node at right atrium

options.A: Lower end of crista terminalis next to the inferior vena cava
options.C: Upper end of crista terminalis next to the superior vena cava

16-17 MEDU2300 Final Q9.explanation: Mature atrium is formed by rough primordial atrium with pectinate muscles, and the smooth sinus venarum formed by resorption of large veins. The division between them is the crista terminalis, which is a smooth muscular ridge acting as the origin of pectinate muscles. The SA node is at the superior end of crista terminalis next to SVC.

16-17 MEDU2300 Final Q10.question: Regarding the Conduction system, which is correct?

options.A: AV node is between right auricle and svc
options.B: AV node is just above coronary sinus
options.C: Bundle of his is anterior to interventricular septum
options.D: Moderator band is in left atrium
options.E: Moderator band carried left bundle fibres

16-17 MEDU2300 Final Q10.explanation: A is describing the position of the SA node. Bundle of His runs within the IV septum. Moderator band arises from IV septum to anterior papillary muscle of RV.

17-18 MEDU2300 Test Q3.question: Which of the following structure pass through the middle mediastinum
17-18 MEDU2300 Test Q3.explanation:
From Moore’s Essential Clinical Anatomy: The middle mediastinum coincides with the pericardium, containing the heart, ascending aorta, pulmonary trunk, SVC, arch of azygos vein, and main bronchi.
Middle mediastinum refers to the space occupied by the pericardium. Phrenic nerve runs on the anterior surface of fibrous pericardium and innervates it, then descends to the diaphragm. Therefore, the phrenic nerve traverses the middle mediastinum.
From BRS Gross Anatomy: Middle mediastinum: - Lies between right and left pleural cavities - Contains the heart, pericardium, phrenic nerves, roots of great vessels (aorta, pulmonary arteries & veins, and vena cava), arch of azygos vein and main bronchi
Comment: If middle mediastinum = pericardium and contents of pericardial sac, phrenic nerve seems to make more sense since it is embedded in pericardium, so I would choose C (according to my understanding azygos vein is mainly in posterior mediastinum and enters SVC in superior mediastinum)
In Franky’s world: A: posterior mediastinum; C: posterior mediastinum; D: posterior mediastinum. But he didn’t mention whether phrenic nerve belongs to middle mediastinum (Lecture slide P.5)

![](img/17-18_MEDU2300_Test_Q3_1775809752095.png)

17-18 MEDU2300 Test Q4.question: Which of the following statements concerning physical examination of the heart is correct?

options.A: Apex beat is displaced medially in most patient with cardiac defect
options.B: apex beat is found in the 5th intercostal space at the anterior axillary line
options.C: malfunctioning of heart valve results in murmur
options.D: mitral valve can be heard at lower left sternal border
options.E: sound of pulmonary valve is projected on the right 2nd intercostal space

17-18 MEDU2300 Test Q4.explanation:
A: shd be lateral/inferior but not medially
B: mid-clavicular line
D: mitral → tricuspid; mitral valve can be heard at 5th intercostal space at mid-clavicular line
E: pulmonary valve can be auscultated at left sternal border of 2nd ics

![](img/17-18_MEDU2300_Test_Q4_1775809843288.png)

17-18 MEDU2300 Final Q2.question: A 10 year old boy has difficulty in breathing after swallowing a peanut. Which is the most likely place the peanut lodged in?
17-18 MEDU2300 Final Q2.explanation: Right main bronchus is shorter, wider and more vertical when compared to the left. This makes foreign bodies that enter through the trachea more likely to be lodged in this bronchus or the inferior lobar bronchus.

17-18 MEDU2300 Final Q3.question: What is above the right lung hilum
17-18 MEDU2300 Final Q3.explanation:
A: right above right lung hilum
Note that the root of the lungs are about T5-T7 level.

17-18 MEDU2300 Final Q4.question: Which of the following structures pass through aortic hiatus of diaphragm

17-18 MEDU2300 Test Q6.question: Anatomy of lung and pleura

options.A: Cardiac notch is not present in left pleura
options.B: Apex of the lung extends from the superior thoracic aperture
options.C: Horizontal fissure meets the oblique fissure at the level of 6th rib
options.D: The inferior border of the lung the meet the midclavicular line at the 8th rib
options.E: The inferior border of the left pleura extends to the inferior thoracic aperture

17-18 MEDU2300 Test Q6.explanation:
A is obviously wrong.
B is correct as the apex of lungs extends beyond 1st ribs.
Horizontal and oblique fissures in the right lung meet at 5th rib at mid-axillary line.
Inferior border of the lung meets the midclavicular line at the 6th rib instead.
Inferior limit of pleura never extends beyond the inferior aperture, which is bound by the diaphragm.

17-18 MEDU2300 Test Q7.question: Which of the following correctly describes the caval opening of the diaphragm?

options.A: It is narrowed by the stimulation of vagus nerve
options.B: It circles the left phrenic nerve and inferior vena cava
options.C: It is at the level of T10.
options.D: It is found in the central tendon of the diaphragm
options.E: It lies posterior to and to the left of the aortic orifice.

17-18 MEDU2300 Test Q7.explanation:
A is wrong as caval opening is only under control of diaphragm movements, for example its contraction during inspiration will enlarge the caval opening on central tendon, hence dilating IVC to facilitate venous return. You may say that it is dilated under stimulation of the phrenic nerve, or narrowed by the stimulation of the pneumotaxic centre in rostral pons (HUS2 Respiratory physiology).
B is wrong as it should be the right phrenic nerve passing through the caval opening to reach the back of liver.
C is wrong as it is at T8 level.
D is correct.
E is wrong as aortic hiatus is at the midline of posterior margin of diaphragm, while caval opening is on the right and anterior aspect of it as IVC is on the right of abdominal aorta below.

17-18 MEDU2300 Test Q13.question: When standing, fluid in the pleural cavity will accumulate in which region?
17-18 MEDU2300 Test Q13.explanation: E is the only correct answer as costodiaphragmatic recess is on the lateral and inferior corner of pleural cavity, which is unoccupied by the lungs during respiratory movements. Thoracentesis can be performed at 9th ICS at midaxillary line to access the costodiaphragmatic recess, draining pleural fluid for further investigations.

18-19 MEDU2300 Test Q3.question: Apart from esophagus, which of the following passes through esophageal hiatus?

18-19 MEDU2300 Test Q4.question: Which of the following nerves supply the costal parietal pleura?
18-19 MEDU2300 Test Q4.explanation:
(Refers to small moore P.59)
Muscular branches arise all along the course of the intercostal nerves to supply the intercostal, subcostal, transversus thoracis, levatores costarum, and serratus posterior muscles (Table 1.2) and sensory branches pass to the parietal pleura.
Refer to BRS Gross Anatomy P. 58, The parietal pleura is innervated by the intercostal nerves (costal pleura and the peripheral portion of the diaphragmatic pleura), and the phrenic nerves (central portion of the diaphragmatic pleura and the mediastinal pleura).
Parietal pleura are sensitive to pain.
Don’t be tricked by the phrenic nerve!

18-19 MEDU2300 Final Q1.question: surface anatomy: where can one locate the horizontal fissure of the right lung along the mid-axillary line?
18-19 MEDU2300 Final Q1.explanation:
![](img/18-19_MEDU2300_Final_Q1_1775809926507.jpeg)
![](img/18-19_MEDU2300_Final_Q1_1775809930636.png)

According to the “Surface anatomy of Chest” lecture given by Dr. Calvin Ng, the horizontal fissure begins at the 4th rib at the sternal border and runs to the 5th rib at midaxillary line. You may also obtain similar facts from Gray’s anatomy.
Course of horizontal fissure (right lung): From 4th rib sternal border, to mid-axillary line at 5th rib level
Course of oblique fissure (both lungs): From spinous process of T3 to mid-axillary line at 5th rib level, to mid-clavicular line at 6th rib.

18-19 MEDU2300 Test Q5.question: Which of the following coronary artery branches is related to myocardial infarction in anterior interventricular septum?
18-19 MEDU2300 Test Q5.explanation: A. LAD = left anterior descending artery = anterior interventricular artery

18-19 MEDU2300 Test Q6.question: With regards to coronary conduction, which of the following is correct?

options.A: The bundle of His runs anterior to the septum
options.B: The AV node is located at the junction between right atrium and SVC
options.C: The AV node is located next to the opening of coronary sinus
options.D: The septomarginal band is located at left ventricle

18-19 MEDU2300 Final Q2.question: Brachiocephalic trunk which vertebrae level?
18-19 MEDU2300 Final Q2.explanation:
![](img/18-19_MEDU2300_Final_Q2_1775809959622.png)

Inferior end and main bulk of aortic arch is at T4/T5 level behind sternal angle, while the top of arch (Superior border) can reach the T3/T4 level. Therefore, you may consider T3 as the level of origin of all 3 large arteries from aortic arch.

18-19 MEDU2300 Final Q4.question: Which level does the azygos vein enter the superior vena cava?
18-19 MEDU2300 Final Q4.explanation:
Azygos vein arches over the hilum of the right lung to enter SVC, hence above the right main bronchus. Note that the right main bronchus enters the lung at T5 (Left is T6, as the left bronchus is longer and the right lung is being pushed above by the liver), so the azygos vein must course just above T5. T4 is the best answer here.
External Reference: https://radiopaedia.org/articles/azygos-venous-system

19-20 MEDU2300 Test Q3.question: what nerve provides main motor innervation to the diaphragm:

options.A: Cervical sympathetic plexus and phrenic nerve
options.B: Greater splanchnic nerves and phrenic nerves
options.C: Lesser splanchnic nerves and phrenic nerves
options.D: Lower intercostal nerves and phrenic nerves

19-20 MEDU2300 Final Q3.question: Which of the following structures passes through the esophageal hiatus?

19-20 MEDU2300 Final Q4.question: A tumor mass is found to be located in the vicinity of the cardiac notch. Which of the following structures would most likely have to be excised in the lung resection?

options.A: Lower (inferior) lobe of the right lung
options.B: Upper (superior) lobe of the right lung
options.C: Lower lobe of the left lung
options.D: Upper lobe of the left lung
options.E: Middle lobe of the right lung

19-20 MEDU2300 Final Q4.explanation:
Note the following special features of the left lung:
- Lingula
  - Tongue-shaped portion of the upper lobe that corresponds to the middle lobe of the right lung
- Cardiac notch
  - A deep indentation of the anterior border of the superior lobe of left lung
 
*Middle Lobe of right 全部消失變成 superior lobe in left！
Look at the arrangement of the upper lobe of the left lung.

![](img/19-20_MEDU2300_Final_Q4_1775810019755.png)

19-20 MEDU2300 Final Q5.question: A tumor located just superior to the root of the right lung may block or reduce blood flow in which of the following vessels?

19-20 MEDU2300 Final Q6.question: in Angina pectoris, radiated pain from inner surface of Left Arm is mediated through sensor fibres running in

options.D: 4) recurrent laryngeal nerve and left vagus nerve
options.E: 5) sensory peripheral nerve at T1-T4 dermatomes

19-20 MEDU2300 Final Q6.explanation: No pain is sensed from E, just illusion

20-21 MEDU2300 Test Q3.question: Which of the nerve supply the costal parietal pleura

20-21 MEDU2300 Test Q4.question: Which statement is true about the right main bronchus

options.A: it branches to 2 lobar branches near the hilum
options.C: it is inferior to arch of azygos vein
options.D: it is longer and more horizontal than left bronchus

20-21 MEDU2300 Test Q4.explanation:
A. The right main bronchus branches into 3 lobar branches (superior, middle and inferior), while the left main bronchus only branches into 2 (upper and lower).
B. Only the left main bronchus crosses anterior to the esophagus
C. Refer to the first figure below (This is one of the MOST important / commonly asked landmarks in this block, should have known after going through that many questions.)
D. The right main bronchus is shorter, wider and more vertical than the left bronchus.

![](img/20-21_MEDU2300_Test_Q4_1775810089417.jpeg)

20-21 MEDU2300 Final Q3.question: The hiatus on the central tendon on the diaphragm contains which of the following structures?
20-21 MEDU2300 Final Q3.explanation:
![](img/20-21_MEDU2300_Final_Q3_1775810105316.png)

This is hard though. Hiatus on central tendon refers to caval opening at T8 level, as aortic hiatus is at posterior end of diaphragm formed by median arcuate ligament, while esophageal hiatus is formed by right crus of diaphragm. It is the right phrenic nerve accompanying IVC to traverse the opening. A and D traverse through aortic hiatus, while B and E traverse through esophageal hiatus.
Well, if you studied the previous questions well, your photographic memory should now tell you everything written in words here.

20-21 MEDU2300 Test Q5.question: Which of the following is correct?

options.A: The anterior interventricular artery is a major branch of left coronary artery
options.C: Both coronary arteries receive blood during systole(?)
options.D: Most people have their posterior interventricular artery from left coronary artery
options.E: The distance traveled before branching off by right coronary artery is shorter than that by left coronary artery

20-21 MEDU2300 Test Q5.explanation:
A is clearly correct.
C is wrong because during systole, the cusps of the aortic valve are pressed against the opening of coronary arteries and there is no blood entering them. You will also learn in HUF that systole of the heart compresses the coronary arteries and the flow could be temporarily stopped.
D is obviously wrong, as the LAD is most likely originating from LCA.
E is wrong as well, as LCA is much shorter than the right, given that it immediately branches into LCX and LAD.

20-21 MEDU2300 Final Q5.question: Blood clot blocks anterior interventricular artery, which of the following is affected?
20-21 MEDU2300 Final Q5.explanation:
Anterior surface of the right ventricle is supplied by the right marginal artery.
Inferior part of the interventricular septum can be supplied by the posterior interventricular artery.
The best choice here is A, because LAD comes from the left coronary artery.

20-21 MEDU2300 Final Q6.question: The atrioventricular groove or sulcus of the heart contains the:

options.C: marginal branch of the right coronary artery

20-21 MEDU2300 Final Q6.explanation:
This is different from the interventricular groove.
Note the course of the right coronary artery that travels to the right side of the heart almost horizontally.

20-21 MEDU2300 Final Q7.question: Hemiazygos vein, after going through the aortic hiatus, travels posterior to which of the following structures before joining with the azygos vein?
20-21 MEDU2300 Final Q7.explanation:
Such question shows how Maksim (@medbook.pro) describes thorax with 易學難精, basically there are too many details to study. To get the answer directly, refer to lecture notes P. 28, which the diagram explains everything (but you will never realize that this is also an important point to note for exams…)
Hemiazygos vein ascends on the left side of the aorta, of course irrelevant to IVC and right phrenic nerve in caval opening. Vagus nerve runs along the oesophagus and is not related to the hemiazygos vein at such a low position too (T7/T8 for hemiazygos vein to drain into azygos vein).
The remaining two choices are virtually impossible to imagine. From Netter, the sympathetic trunks are lateral to azygos and hemiazygos veins, so do the splanchnic nerves. However, there is still some dispute over the relation of the thoracic duct with the hemiazygos vein. But I can assure you that the hemiazygos vein crosses the thoracic duct as it runs right to meet the azygos vein. Franky’s slides and Netter say hemiazygos vein runs posterior to thoracic duct, while Moore says otherwise. Please trust Franky for the exam, as it is him setting the exam question.
Revisit: Big Moore says the same as Franky, but not the same as Small Moore :o

21-22 MEDU2300 Test Q3.question: Which of the following is true about the left main bronchus?

options.B: It is shorter and more vertical than right main bronchus
options.C: It passes in front of the esophagus
options.D: The arch of azygos vein is superior to it
options.E: It travels in the superior mediastinum

21-22 MEDU2300 Test Q3.explanation:
A is clearly wrong as the left lung only has 2 lobes.
B is just the opposite.
C is correct because the trachea is always anterior to the esophagus, while the oesophagus is slightly displaced to the left in later courses. Such a position is also one of the constriction sites of the esophagus.
D is true for the right main bronchus, as azygos vein arches over the right hilum to drain into IVC anteriorly.
E is wrong, by the time the trachea divides into left and right bronchi, it has already entered to middle mediastinum. Note that the division between superior and lower 3 mediastinal compartments is the transverse thoracic plane at sternal angle (T4/5 level), hence the main bronchi will not be in superior mediastinum anymore.

21-22 MEDU2300 Test Q4.question: Which of the following structure pass through the oesophageal hiatus?

21-22 MEDU2300 Final Q3.question: The aortic hiatus of the diaphragm transmits the following structures except:
21-22 MEDU2300 Final Q3.explanation:
C could be correct, as splanchnic nerves do not traverse any openings according to Netter, but Franky’s slides say otherwise. I would prefer believing Franky.
However, the vagus nerve must traverse oesophageal hiatus and must be correct. Just pick the vagus nerve next time you see this question. Sometimes studying too hard is an issue...

21-22 MEDU2300 Final Q4.question: Inhaled foreign body is most likely to go to right lung. All statements can explain why except:

options.A: Right lung is larger and wider than left lung
options.B: Right main bronchus enters lung root at T5
options.C: Right main bronchus larger than left main bronchus
options.D: Right main bronchus is more vertical then left main bronchus
options.E: Right main bronchus is shorter than left main bronchus

21-22 MEDU2300 Final Q4.explanation:
A is a correct claim. Right lung is larger (3 lobes), shorter and wider (Liver compresses from below), but this is irrelevant to entry of foreign objects to the lungs. B-E are all valid comparisons of left and right main bronchi. Just make sure you don’t mix up right and left bronchi with left or right lungs.
C-E are all very valid reasons to support the situation, while B seems not. B is the reason why the right main bronchus is shorter and hence easier for an object to enter the right lung.

21-22 MEDU2300 Test Q5.question: Which of the following artery bypass the coronary sinus?
21-22 MEDU2300 Test Q5.explanation: E does not exist for your reference. Small cardiac vein runs along the right marginal artery and then RCA in the coronary sulcus. Middle cardiac vein runs along PDA, while the great cardiac vein runs along LAD and LCX. Anterior cardiac vein is the only exception draining minor blood from the right ventricle directly into the right atrium. (Alternative name is smallest cardiac vein).

21-22 MEDU2300 Test Q6.question: Which of the following is true for thoracic duct?

options.A: It ascends to posterior mediastinum behind esophagus and to the left of the aorta
options.B: It starts from cisterna chyli in the abdomen and terminates at the junction of right subclavian and internal jugular vein
options.C: It drains the whole body except the right thorax, right upper limb, and right half of the head and neck
options.D: It traverses the diaphragm through esophageal hiatus
options.E: It joins the right jugular and subclavian trunk

21-22 MEDU2300 Test Q6.explanation: Thoracic duct ascends on the right of aorta, and the left of azygos vein. Thoracic duct indeed starts from cisterna chyli at L1/L2 level, but should end at junction of left subclavian and internal jugular veins. Thoracic duct traverses the aortic hiatus.

21-22 MEDU2300 Final Q5.question: Occlusion of anterior interventricular artery by blood clots is most likely to lead to infarction of
21-22 MEDU2300 Final Q5.explanation:
A. Correct
B. Right marginal artery
C. Right marginal artery + Posterior interventricular artery
D. Left circumflex artery
E. Posterior interventricular artery

21-22 MEDU2300 Final Q6.question: The following veins drain to the coronary sinus except

21-22 MEDU2300 Final Q7.question: The heart receives sensory fibers from which of the following vertebral levels of sympathetic trunk
21-22 MEDU2300 Final Q7.explanation: Always remember that T1 is involved or else there will not be referred pain over the inner surface of the left arm. This is because the medial cutaneous nerve of the arm arises from the T1 spinal segment. The dermatome of T2 is already in the chest and axilla.

22-23 MEDU2300 Test Q3.question: Which of the following is the most accurate description for the innervation of phrenic nerve?
22-23 MEDU2300 Test Q3.explanation: Phrenic nerve supplies the motor action of diaphragm, and sensation over central tendon of diaphragm and pericardium. (This is a question that appeared in the formative assessments!)

22-23 MEDU2300 Test Q4.question: CT scan shows a tumour superior to the right root of lung. Which blood circulation will most likely be affected?

22-23 MEDU2300 Test Q20.question: Where is the seromucous gland of the trachea located?
22-23 MEDU2300 Test Q20.explanation:
This is like the position of glands in the esophagus and duodenum.
Note that this is supposed to be a histology question.

22-23 MEDU2300 Test Q5.question: Which of the following branches of coronary arteries is most likely to be involved in myocardial infarction affecting the posterior aspect of IV septum?

22-23 MEDU2300 Test Q6.question: According to the cardiac conduction system, which of the following is correct?

options.A: Atrioventricular bundle (of His) normally runs anterior to the membranous interventricular septum.
options.B: Atrioventricular node is located at the junction of the superior vena cava and right auricle.
options.C: Atrioventricular node is located next to the opening of the coronary sinus.
options.D: The septomarginal trabecula is located at the left ventricle.
options.E: Septomarginal trabecula carries left conducting fibres.

FA MEDU2300 Thorax Q7.question: Which one of the following statements regarding the phrenic nerve is MOST ACCURATE?

options.D: It supplies mainly the diaphragm and pericardium
options.E: It supplies mainly the skin of the chest.

FA MEDU2300 Thorax Q7.explanation: Phrenic nerve gives motor supply for the diaphragm, and sensory supply of the central portion of diaphragm and entire pericardium (fibrous & parietal).

FA MEDU2300 Thorax Q8.question: Which one of the following statements concerning the lung is CORRECT?

options.A: Lingula is the extension of the right superior lung lobe.
options.B: Apex of the lung extends above the first rib.
options.C: Horizontal and oblique fissures of the right lung usually meet at the midclavicular line.
options.D: Phrenic nerve goes down to the diaphragm on the anterior surface of the lung.
options.E: Horizontal fissure is a feature of the left lung.

FA MEDU2300 Thorax Q8.explanation: Lingula is the analogue of the middle lobe growing in the superior lobe of the left lung. Horizontal and oblique fissure meet at the 5th ribs on the midaxillary line, instead of the midclavicular line. Phrenic nerve runs on the anterior aspect of lung hila, but not the surface, as it is traversing in anterior mediastinum to supply the pericardium.

FA MEDU2300 Thorax Q9.question: Which one of the following structures transmits through the aortic hiatus?
FA MEDU2300 Thorax Q9.explanation:
Note that E is also an answer. 

A travels together through oesophageal hiatus, while B and C travels through caval opening.

FA MEDU2300 Thorax Q10.question: Which one of the following structures is related to the mediastinal surface of the right lung?
FA MEDU2300 Thorax Q10.explanation: This is just asking what is on the left of the right lung, and of course is SVC and right heart border. This is often asked in spot tests and you may find a similar question in the file.

FA MEDU2300 Thorax Q11.question: Which one of the following pleurae is insensitive to pain?
FA MEDU2300 Thorax Q11.explanation: Visceral pleura is innervated by ANS, which only sends vague visceral afferents to spinal cords as pain signal. On the contrary, parietal pleura (A+B+D) is innervated by intercostal nerves, which transmits sharp pain signals.

FA MEDU2300 Thorax Q12.question: With reference to the trachea in the thoracic cavity, which one of the following statements is CORRECT?

options.A: It terminates at vertebral level T6.
options.B: It is posterior to the oesophagus.
options.C: It has the recurrent laryngeal nerve on its left but not right side.
options.D: It is posterior to the left atrium.
options.E: Its lower right side is in contact with the aortic arch.

FA MEDU2300 Thorax Q12.explanation: Trachea bifurcates at the sternal angle, at the level of T4/T5 IV disc, which is already the level of aortic arch, above the entire heart. It is anterior to the oesophagus, and its lower left side is being arched over by the aortic arch. C is correct as right recurrent laryngeal nerve hooks around the right subclavian artery, which is beyond the thorax.

FA MEDU2300 Thorax Q13.question: Which of the following statements concerning the pericardium is CORRECT?

options.A: The visceral portion of the serous pericardium covers the heart only.
options.B: The pericardium is supplied directly by internal thoracic arteries.
options.C: The fibrous pericardium is a closed sac having two layers.
options.D: The fibrous pericardium is innervated by phrenic nerves.
options.E: The parietal portion of the serous pericardium covers the diaphragm.

FA MEDU2300 Thorax Q13.explanation:
Visceral portion of serous pericardium (Epicardium) also covers the root of great vessels.
Pericardium is supplied by the pericardiacophrenic artery from the internal thoracic artery.
Fibrous pericardium is just a fibrous sheath with no space within, but the serous pericardium is. It should be fibrous pericardium covering the diaphragm.

FA MEDU2300 Thorax Q14.question: Which of the following statements concerning the sinoatrial (SA) node is CORRECT?

options.A: It is receptive to sympathetic innervation only.
options.B: It is located on the atrial wall near the tricuspid valve.
options.C: It has an arterial supply arising from the right coronary artery.
options.D: It lies near the entrance of the inferior vena cava to the heart.
options.E: It generates an electrical signal that immediately causes the heart ventricles to contract.

FA MEDU2300 Thorax Q14.explanation: SA node is near the opening of SVC, while the AV node is near the opening of the coronary sinus. C is correct as SA and AV nodes are in the right atrium, and there are nodal branches arising from RCA for them. E is wrong as the electrical signal has to pass through the internodal pathway, AV node, bundle of His, and Purkinje fibres to reach the cardiomyocytes in ventricles.

FA MEDU2300 Thorax Q15.question: With the heart in its usual position in the body:

options.A: The ascending aorta lies anterior to the pulmonary trunk.
options.B: The left ventricle forms most of the anterior surface.
options.C: The base of the heart consists mainly of the right atrium.
options.D: The left atrium can be seen on the anterior aspect.
options.E: The right border is formed by the right atrium.

FA MEDU2300 Thorax Q15.explanation: Ascending aorta is on the right of the pulmonary trunk. It is the right ventricle forming the most of the anterior surface. It is the left atrium forming the base, which can only be seen from the back. Right border is indeed formed by the right atrium, same in CXR. (Lmao better learn the heart borders in case it appears in the radiology spot exam.)

FA MEDU2300 Thorax Q16.question: At which of the following sites can the azygos vein be located?

options.B: Superior to the right lung root.
options.C: On the posterior thoracic wall left to the vertebral column.
options.E: Inferior to the right lung root.

FA MEDU2300 Thorax Q18.question: Which of the following has a close contact with the esophagus in the posterior mediastinum?
FA MEDU2300 Thorax Q18.explanation:
Left atrium is the base of the heart, receiving 4 pulmonary veins from the hila. Recall that pulmonary veins are more anterior to the bronchus in the hilum, the left atrium is separated from the esophagus by the carina and bronchi.
Although trachea runs just anterior to the esophagus for most of their courses since their division at the pharynx, it only traverses the superior mediastinum. Its bifurcation and two bronchi are in the middle mediastinum, together forming the anterior border of posterior mediastinum.
C is wrong as it stays on the right and does not contact the esophagus slightly on the left of the midline.
D is correct as the aortic arch runs posteriorly on the left of trachea and esophagus as it becomes the thoracic aorta.
E is wrong as the thyroid gland is anterior to the thyroid and cricoid cartilages at C4-C6 levels (Not in posterior mediastinum), and separated from the esophagus by the trachea.

FA MEDU2300 Thorax Q19.question: The manubrium of the sternum is located in which of the following divisions of the mediastinum?

Extra MEDU2300 Thorax Q2.question: Which of the following about the esophagus is correct?

options.B: The left atrium is located posterior to the esophagus

Extra MEDU2300 Thorax Q2.explanation:
Appreciate the location of constriction, relations of the esophagus to other structures, and the clinical significance of the constriction sites through this question. Seems like there are few, if not no, questions that are set on these concepts before.
A. The thoracic aorta is located to the LEFT of the esophagus.
B. The left atrium is located ANTERIOR to the esophagus.
C. Middle esophageal constriction is also called thoracic constriction, located at the point where the left bronchus crosses the aortic arch inferiorly (to the left of the esophagus), which is at T4. T10 is the lower esophageal constriction, or the diaphragmatic constriction, which is also where the esophagus enters the diaphragm at the esophageal opening.
D. Correct. This is one of the clinical significance of these constriction sites. Another important point to note is that the walls of these sites are more prone to be injured by sharp / pointed objects if swallowed.
E. The origin of the upper esophageal constriction (pharyngo-esophageal constriction) is located BELOW the pharynx.

17-18 MEDU2600 Final Q1.question: Which of the following is the property of channel protein?

options.E: the rate of transport is lower than carrier protein

17-18 MEDU2600 Final Q1.explanation:
It is important to know the similarities and differences between channel protein and carrier protein.
[Specificity]
Channel proteins transport specific molecules depending on their size, shape and charge. It is correct to say that it is highly specific. At the same time, to transport a particle through carrier proteins, that particle must bind onto the specific sites on carrier proteins. The specificity is highest.
[Speed]
After binding, a conformational change is required in carrier proteins to move the particles to the other side of the membrane. Yet, in channel proteins, this is not the case as the particles move directly through the channels. The rate of conduction in channel proteins (10^8 particles per second) is much higher than that of carrier proteins (10^4 particles per second). E is wrong!

21-22 MEDU2600 Final Q9.question: Which of the following is a characteristic of a metabolic pathway?

options.A: Catabolic and anabolic pathways are completely reversible
options.B: Catabolic and Anabolic pathways are controlled independently
options.C: Enzymes are always activated through phosphorylation
options.D: Enzymes are always inhibited through phosphorylation
options.E: The first step is the committed step of metabolic pathway

21-22 MEDU2600 Final Q9.explanation:
This question is rather interesting given its requirement of clear concepts. A is false as both pathways are irreversible, or else the metabolites will be flowing around without a clear direction. C and D are false as it depends on the structure of enzyme. E is seemingly true but actually false. I can give you two examples for this, and even if the statement becomes "First irreversible step", it is still false.

The first example is conversion of glucose into G6P. Although it is the first irreversible step in glycolysis, G6P can still be utilised in different pathways, e.g. glycogenesis, PPP, and it is not the first metabolite that can only proceed into glycolysis. It will be the conversion F6P into F-1,6-BP, as this is the next irreversible step giving metabolites that can only continue and complete the glycolytic reactions.

Another example is conversion of ribose 5-phosphate (R5P) into phosphoriboysl pyrophsphate (PRPP). Although it is the first step of de novo purine synthesis, it is also not the committed step. This is because PRPP can be also used in salvage reaction to produce purine nucleotides by HGPRT or APRT, or synthesis of OMP from orotate in de novo pyrimidine synthesis. The committed step here is formation of phosphoribosyl 1-amine from PRPP and glutamine.

21-22 MEDU2600 Test Q50.question: Which of the following lipids contains acid?
21-22 MEDU2600 Test Q50.explanation:
For simplicity, just refer to the first page of this document. The first 2 lectures are just about rote memorization, and you should recite as little as you could within our syllabus. Even if you understand all the following there won’t be extra marks. Just recite it, and pass it, and that’s it.
Ganglioside = Glycolipid + Sialic ACID
You will need the basic knowledge about glycolipids and sphingolipids before you can answer this question. We can begin with the formation of sphinganine from serine and palmitoyl CoA, and then into ceramide after receiving another acyl CoA group. Sphingosine refers to the part of ceramide excluding the foreign acyl CoA. TAG is too simple to be discussed here. Ganglioside and globoside are both sphingolipids originating from ceramide, but the difference lies in the oligosaccharide residues attached to the lipid. Attaching 1 sugar residue yields a cerebroside, while multiple residues yield a globoside. Ganglioside is further formed from globoside by attaching a sialic acid residue, hence is the only answer here. It is quite hard to know such detail at first glance, but the later lecture featuring various IEM of lipids should contain a figure illustrating various lipids and possible IEM. It is a good concept reminder for you to link up the function and structure of different lipids.

C. Surfactants are synthesized by type II alveolar cells.
D. True. Recite this. (FYI: actually PAF is derived from ether analog of PC, instead of ordinary ester form, i.e. it is an alkyl group on C1 instead of an acyl group.)
E. False. The L/S ratio is low in early gestation, indicating immature fetal lungs. You can link this with the fact that lecithin is one of the chief components in lung surfactant secreted after week 24, near the end of canalicular period (week 16-26).

A is clearly false as FA are always carried in the form of acyl CoA.
B is false either as lipogenesis should be active in the fed state.
C is possible but very inefficient, and there is no need to construct glycerol 3P from amino acids either, we can obtain it simply from DHAP or dietary TAG.
D is correct as this is one method to obtain glycerol 3-phosphate. This is because NADH is consumed. Another method is direct phosphorylation of glycerol by glycerol kinase. However, this reaction is only possible in the liver. But if we consider using glycerol 3-phosphate for TAG synthesis, then it is possible everywhere.

21-22 MEDU2600 Final Q11.question: The pulmonary surfactant produced by alveolar type 2 cell contain:
21-22 MEDU2600 Final Q11.explanation: Be careful although lecithin/phosphatidylcholine is the chief component, there is also some sphingomyelin. As a further elaboration, have you ever thought about why L and S are used as lung surfactants? Recall that L and S are just phosphocholine attached to DAG and ceramide backbones. You can observe that the alkyl backbone of them forms the hydrophobic tail, while the phosphocholine group forms the hydrophilic head. This is very much similar to the case of bile acids forming lipid droplets and micelles, but not for emulsification this time, but to reduce the surface tension of the water film on the surface of alveoli. The charged heads of the lipids repel each other to help spread the water film, instead of coalescing together as a water droplet, hence lowering the surface tension to prevent collapse of alveoli during expiration.

The first step is oxidation of fatty acid (Removal of H) and cytochrome b5, by molecular oxygen, catalyzed by fatty acyl CoA desaturase. Oxidised cytochrome b5 is then regenerated by consuming NADH, catalyzed by cytochrome b5 reductase. As oxygen is reduced in this reaction, of course water is formed.

17-18 MEDU2600 Test Q53.question: Which of the following is the mechanism of cholestyramine for treatment of hypercholesterolemia?

options.A: Activate biosynthesis of bile acid directly
options.B: Activate biosynthesis of steroid hormone directly
options.C: Increase secretion of CCK and secretin
options.D: Inhibit absorption of bile acid in distal GI lumen

17-18 MEDU2600 Test Q53.explanation: Cholestyramine carries positively charged resin that binds onto negatively charged bile acid. The bile acid fails to be absorbed at distal ileum. Liver will have to utilize circulating cholesterol for new bile acid synthesis. Since more bile is lost and more cholesterol is used by the liver, cholesterol levels drop.

17-18 MEDU2600 Final Q15.question: tangier disease mutation
17-18 MEDU2600 Final Q15.explanation:
A. Cholesterol in peripheral cells cannot be uptook by HDL molecules. This may impair cellular functions and lead to symptoms such as neuropathy and orange-coloured tonsils.
B. Leptin is a hormone that is released in proportion to the number of adipocytes to generate a sense of fullness (reduce appetite). Usually, in obese patients, they have a high level of circulating leptin, but the response to the hormone is limited.
C. Hexoamidase A deficiency is related to Tay-Sach’s disease. Hydrolysis of GM2 fails, so that it accumulates in cells. Symptoms include a cherry-red spot on the retina, as well as loss of motor function in newborns.
D. Sphingomyelinase deficiency is related to Niemann-pick disease. Sphingomyelin cannot be broken down into phosphocholine and ceramide. Symptoms include neurodegeneration, hepatomegaly and splenomegaly.
E. Glucocerebrosidase deficiency is related to Gaucher’s disease, which fails to break glucocerebroside down into glucose and cerebroside. Symptoms include hepatomegaly, splenomegaly, accumulation of glucocerebroside in bone marrow and pancytopenia.

Niemann-Pick disease: Deficiency in sphingomyelinase

Tay-Sachs disease: Deficiency in hexosaminidase A → Reduced breakdown of GM2 in gangliosides → lost of motor function (in infants) + Cherry-red spot in eyes (embolism)

21-22 MEDU2600 Test Q54.question: Leptin is an adipocytic hormone which

options.A: Acts on our hypothalamic neurons to increase appetite
options.B: Counteract the effect of peptide YY on gastric emptying
options.C: Can cross the blood brain barrier
options.E: Leptin is inversely proportional to fat

21-22 MEDU2600 Test Q54.explanation: Leptin is secreted by adipose tissues, which acts on hypothalamus to reduce appetite and produce satiety. At least you should discard A and E first. CCK and peptide YY inhibit gastric emptying after meal and also causes satiety at the hypothalamus. Hence, these two hormones are synergistic with leptin in terms of producing satiety. However, leptin will not affect gastric emptying. Leptin can cross the blood brain barrier at circumventricular organs of the hypothalamus, which is necessary for it to act on hypothalamic nuclei to cause satiety. Leptin also exhibits diurnal variation as its secretion is highest during midnight sleep and lowest at the morning. So I will say both C and D are correct, and I am not sure which answer is preferred by Dr. Po Yeung! There is one point you can consider, as the lecture says that leptin is responsible for long-term regulation and seems to emphasize that the level of leptin is mainly determined by size of adipose tissues, instead of daily circadian rhythm. So, you may want to consider C as a better answer.
